mcqs.question: In recursive descent parsing, each nonterminal in the grammar is implemented as a:
mcqs.explanation: In recursive descent parsing, each nonterminal in the grammar has a corresponding recursive procedure (function). The procedure body implements the production rules for that nonterminal. When a nonterminal appears on the right side of a production, its procedure is called recursively.

mcqs.question: What is the main disadvantage of a recursive descent parser that uses backtracking?

options.text: It cannot handle any context-free grammar
It may have exponential time complexity due to repeated re-scanning of input
It cannot produce a parse tree

mcqs.explanation: A recursive descent parser with backtracking may try multiple alternatives for each nonterminal. When a chosen production fails, the parser backtracks and tries the next alternative, potentially re-scanning the same input multiple times. This can lead to exponential time complexity in the worst case.

mcqs.question: A predictive recursive descent parser (without backtracking) requires the grammar to be:
mcqs.explanation: A predictive recursive descent parser requires the grammar to be LL(1), meaning it must be parseable by scanning Left-to-right, producing a Leftmost derivation, with 1 symbol of lookahead. This ensures that the parser can always determine the correct production to use without backtracking by examining just the next input symbol.

mcqs.question: Which grammar transformation must be applied before a left-recursive grammar can be used for recursive descent parsing?
mcqs.explanation: Left recursion causes a recursive descent parser to enter an infinite loop because the procedure for a left-recursive nonterminal A (where A -> A alpha) would call itself without consuming any input. Eliminating left recursion by converting to right-recursive form (A -> beta A', A' -> alpha A' | epsilon) resolves this issue.

mcqs.question: In a predictive recursive descent parser, when the current nonterminal has the production A -> epsilon and the lookahead symbol is in FOLLOW(A), the parser should:

options.text: Apply the epsilon production (return without consuming input)
Consume the lookahead symbol and advance the input

mcqs.explanation: When a nonterminal A has an epsilon production and the lookahead symbol is in FOLLOW(A), it means the current input is valid for what comes after A. The parser applies the epsilon production by simply returning from the procedure for A without consuming any input, effectively deriving the empty string.
